entry.title: Drinfeld modular varieties and the Langlands–Kottwitz method

link.title: Drinfeld modular varieties and the Langlands–Kottwitz method

entry.content:
This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim, Yong-Gyu Choi, Juan Daniel, Jeongwoo Park and . Note: Each item below is usually discussed in more than one talk.

List of talks:

  1. Introduction (Wansu Kim)

  2. Honda--Tate theory for abelian varieties (Juan Daniel)

  3. Mod \(p\) points on modular curves (Juan Daniel)

  4. From modular curves to Drinfeld modular varieties (Yong-Gyu Choi)

  5. Honda--Tate theory for Drinfeld modules (Jeongwoo Park)

  6. The Grothendieck–Lefschetz formula and point counting (Jeongwoo Park)

  7. The Satake isomorphism and the fundamental lemma (Jeongwoo Park)

  8. Reductive groups over schemes and Smooth representations (Jaehak Lee)

  9. Pseudo-coefficients of the Steinberg representation (Jaehak Lee): CANCELLED due to schedule conflict

Reference:

  1. Pierre Deligne, La conjecture de Weil. I, Inst. Hautes Études Sci. Publ. Math. (1974), no. 43, 273–307. MR 340258
  2. Eberhard Freitag and Reinhardt Kiehl, Étale cohomology and the Weil conjecture, Ergebnisse der Mathematik und ihrer Grenzgebiete (3) [Results in Mathematics and Related Areas (3)], vol. 13, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 1988, Translated from the German by Betty S. Waterhouse and William C. Waterhouse, With an historical introduction by J. A. Dieudonné. MR 926276
  3. James S. Milne, Étale cohomology, Princeton Mathematical Series, No. 33, Princeton University Press, Princeton, N.J., 1980. MR 559531
  4. James S. Milne, Lectures on Étale cohomology, https://www.jmilne.org/math/CourseNotes/LEC.pdf, March 22, 2013.
  5. The Stacks project authors, The stacks project, https://stacks.math.columbia.edu, 2024.
entry.summary: This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim, Yong-Gyu Choi, Juan Daniel, Jeongwoo Park and . Note: Each item below is usually discussed in more than one talk. List of talks: Introduction (Wansu Kim)

entry.title: Modular forms to Galois representations
entry.content:
This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim's lab members. Note: Each item below is usually discussed in more than one talk.

List of talks:

  1. Recollection on modular forms (Minju Park)

    Abstract: Introduce holomorphic modular forms (if time permits, Maass forms as well). Introduce the Hecke operators on modular forms, and explain some Atkin--Lehner theory.

  2. Modular forms to Galois representations in weight 2 (Yong-Gyu Choi)

    Abstract: Explain the statement of 'modular forms to Galois representations'. Assume that the weight is \(2\). Explain that there exist something called 'modular curves' $Y_1(N)$ and $X_1(N)$ for $N\in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 5}$ (with its moduli description). Introduce its Jacobian and its behavior. Introduce a special case of the Eichler--Shimura isomorphism $S_2(\Gamma_1(N))\oplus \overline{S_2(\Gamma_1(N))} \simeq H^1_{\text{et}}(X_1(N) , \overline{\mathbb{Q}}_{\ell})\otimes_{\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_{\ell}} \mathbb{\C}$. Explain how the Hecke operators for modular forms come from morphisms between modular curves (and its variants). Introduce the Eichler--Shimura relation. Construct the desired Galois representation.

  3. Recollection on ´etale cohomology and algebraic geometry (Yong-Gyu Choi)

    Abstract: Recall basics on étale cohomology including Leray spectral sequence, weak Lefschetz. Discuss \(\pi_1^{\text{et}}(U,u)\), \(\operatorname{Pic}^0\), \(\operatorname{Alb}\) and Néron models. Relate \(\ell\)-adic Tate modules with $H^1_{\text{et}}$. Introduce Weil sheaves.

  4. Sketch (and some details) of the proof of the full Weil conjecture (Jeongwoo Park)

    Abstract: Follow Tony Feng’s notes with selected proofs in [KW01, Chapter 1]. State [Del80, Théorème 1].

  5. Modular curves and modular forms, and proof of 'modular to Galois' (Juan Daniel, Jeongwoo Park and Yong-Gyu Choi)

    Abstract: Define elliptic curves over schemes. Define \(\Gamma_1(N)\)-level structure for them. Describe generalized elliptic curves over algebraically closed fields. Define modular curves \(Y_1(N)\) and \(X_1(N)\) for \(N\in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 5}\) as schemes over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{Z}[1/N])\), and the universal elliptic curve. Prove the Eichler--Shimura isomorphism \(S_k(\Gamma_1(N))\oplus \overline{S_k(\Gamma_1(N))} \simeq \tilde{H}^1_{\text{et}}(Y_1(N) , \operatorname{Sym}^k R^1 f_*\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_{\ell})\otimes_{\overline{\mathbb{Q}}_{\ell}} \mathbb{\C}\). Introduce \(Y_1(N,p)\) and \(X_1(N,p)\). Define the Hecke operators \(T_p\) and \(\langle n \rangle\) geometrically, and recall the Hecke algebra. Prove the Eichler--Shimura relation \(T_p = F+\langle p\rangle V \), \(FV=VF = p^{k+1}\). Introduce the Kuga--Sato varieties in order to reduce to the case of constant sheaves. Construct the desired Galois representation and prove its properties.

Reference:

  1. Pierre Deligne, La conjecture de Weil. I, Inst. Hautes Études Sci. Publ. Math. (1974), no. 43, 273–307. MR 340258
  2. Eberhard Freitag and Reinhardt Kiehl, Étale cohomology and the Weil conjecture, Ergebnisse der Mathematik und ihrer Grenzgebiete (3) [Results in Mathematics and Related Areas (3)], vol. 13, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 1988, Translated from the German by Betty S. Waterhouse and William C. Waterhouse, With an historical introduction by J. A. Dieudonné. MR 926276
  3. James S. Milne, Étale cohomology, Princeton Mathematical Series, No. 33, Princeton University Press, Princeton, N.J., 1980. MR 559531
  4. James S. Milne, Lectures on Étale cohomology, https://www.jmilne.org/math/CourseNotes/LEC.pdf, March 22, 2013.
  5. The Stacks project authors, The stacks project, https://stacks.math.columbia.edu, 2024.
entry.summary: This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim's lab members. Note: Each item below is usually discussed in more than one talk. List of talks: Recollection on modular forms (Minju Park) Abstract: Introduce holomorphic modular forms (if time permits, Maass forms as well). Introduce the Hecke operators on modular forms, and explain some Atkin--Lehner theory.

entry.title: Étale cohomology
entry.content:
This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim's lab members.

List of talks:

  1. Introduction (Yong-Gyu Choi)

    Abstract: Introduce number theoretic applications of étale cohomology. Define sheaves for the local-homeomorphism topology on topological spaces. State equivalence of two sheaf categories. Recall the definition of injective objects and right derived functor (of left exact functor). Define sheaf cohomology of topological spaces. State the comparison theorem for sheaf cohomology and singular cohomology for locally contractible spaces.

  2. Étale morphisms (Yong-Gyu Choi)

    Abstract: Give equivalent definitions of étale morphisms (excluding the structure theorem). Give examples. Explain infinitesimal criterion. Define geometric point and étale neighborhood. Define strictly henselian local ring and strict henselization. Prove topological invariance of étale schemes.

  3. Grothendieck topology and étale sheaves, étale cohomology of fields (Jeongwoo Park)

    Abstract: Define sites and sheaves on a site (please do not introduce sieves). Give the site corresponding to a topological space. Define étale site (whose objects are étale schemes, not arbitrary schemes). Give examples of étale sheaves of sets/groups (representable presheaves including Gm and µn, constant, quasi-coherent). State that the etale site has enough points [Sta24, Tag 04K5]. Prove [Sta24, Tag 04HX]. Prove the Kummer exact sequence. State the Artin–Schreier sequence. Prove the equivalence between sheaves of sets/abelian groups on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\)et and \(\operatorname{Gal}(k^{\operatorname{sep}}/k)\)-sets/modules. Define sheaf cohomology of abelian sheaves on sites. Relate étale cohomology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) and Galois cohomology.

    4. ...

Reference:

  1. Pierre Deligne, La conjecture de Weil. I, Inst. Hautes Études Sci. Publ. Math. (1974), no. 43, 273–307. MR 340258
  2. Eberhard Freitag and Reinhardt Kiehl, Étale cohomology and the Weil conjecture, Ergebnisse der Mathematik und ihrer Grenzgebiete (3) [Results in Mathematics and Related Areas (3)], vol. 13, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 1988, Translated from the German by Betty S. Waterhouse and William C. Waterhouse, With an historical introduction by J. A. Dieudonné. MR 926276
  3. James S. Milne, Étale cohomology, Princeton Mathematical Series, No. 33, Princeton University Press, Princeton, N.J., 1980. MR 559531
  4. James S. Milne, Lectures on Étale cohomology, https://www.jmilne.org/math/CourseNotes/LEC.pdf, March 22, 2013.
  5. The Stacks project authors, The stacks project, https://stacks.math.columbia.edu, 2024.
entry.summary: This seminar is organized by Yong-Gyu Choi. Speakers are Prof. Wansu Kim's lab members. List of talks: Introduction (Yong-Gyu Choi) Abstract: Introduce number theoretic applications of étale cohomology. Define sheaves for the local-homeomorphism topology on topological spaces. State equivalence of two sheaf categories. Recall the definition of injective objects and right derived functor (of left exact functor). Define sheaf cohomology of topological spaces. State the comparison theorem for sheaf cohomology and singular cohomology for locally contractible spaces.

entry.title: Introduction to Higher Algebra
entry.content:
The only speaker of this seminar is Yong-Gyu Choi. Incomplete notes are available here.

List of talks:

  1. Motivating example (Aug. 30th)

    Abstract: This talk is the very first talk for a semester long series on higher algebra. Higher algebra is the study of algebraic objects in spaces, correcting abnormal behavior of classical homological algebra and incorporating spheres into algebra. In this talk, we will see a concrete example of a higher-algebraic structure on singular cochains, and what structures are needed to formalize such structures with concrete diagrams. We assume familiarity with algebraic topology, category theory and homological algebra (at the first year graduate level).

  2. Infinity-categories (Sep. 6th)

    Abstract: We define the notion of infinity-categories and Kan complex using observations from the previous talk. A process, called the nerve construction, producing infinity-categories from usual categories will be introduced and we will set dictionaries between them. Infinity-categories of functors will be introduced as well.

  3. Homotopy coherent nerves (Sep. 13th)

    Abstract: We introduce the homotopy coherent nerve of Kan-enriched categories, and show they give rise to infinity-categories. We also discuss homotopy theory of Kan complexes.

  4. Composition in infinity-categories (Sep. 20th)

    Abstract: We define the notion of homotopies and compositions in infinity-categories, and discuss Joyal's theorem characterizing isomorphisms. We give a way to parametrize all possible compositions for given morphisms. For this, we introduce the notion of left and right lifting properties and employ them to study trivial Kan fibrations. We apply a similar argument to define left/right/Kan fibrations.

  5. Fibrations of infinity-categories (Oct. 4th)

    Abstract: We apply various properties of fibrations of infinity-categories to recover notions in classical category theory. We also introduce the notion of localizations of simplicial sets.

  6. Cocartesian fibrations (Oct. 11th)

    Abstract: We introduce the notion of cocartesian fibrations of simplicial sets, and check several concrete examples. Lurie's classification theorem of cocartesian fibrations, which is the central result of the first half of the series, will be presented without proof. To see how it is applied in concrete way, we introduce limits and colimits in infinity-categories and introduce terminologies regarding them.

  7. Limits (Oct. 25th)

    Abstract: We describe limits and colimits inside the infinity-category of small infinity-categories, using classification of cocartesian fibrations. We define commutative monoid objects and, in particular, symmetric monoidal infinity-categories. We explicitly see how much data is stored in such notions. Lastly, we introduce the notion of Kan extensions and discuss its existence and uniqueness up to contractibility.

  8. Yoneda lemma and Stable infinity-categories (Nov. 1st)

    Abstract: We construct the cartesian symmetric monoidal infinity-category and discuss various phenomena regarding Yoneda embeddings, using the trivial Kan fibrations obtained from left Kan extensions. We now start discussion on higher algebra, starting from the definition of stable infinity-categories.

  9. More on Stable infinity-categories (Nov. 8th)

    Abstract: We discuss basic properties of stable infinity-categories, including t-structure. Using the Dold--Kan correspondence, the derived infinity-category of (nice) abelian category is defined and its universal property is described. Moreover, by recalling the Brown representability theorem, we define the infinity-category of spectra which parametrized generalized cohomology theories on (pointed) topological spaces.

  10. The infinity-category of spectra (Nov. 15th)

    Abstract: We give a purely infinity-categorical construction of Eilenberg--MacLane spaces and spectra, and see how these define singular homology and cohomology. We give another equivalent definition of the infinity-category of spectra, and the Moore spectra are introduced. Lastly, the notion of infinity-operad is defined.

  11. Smash product of spectra (Nov. 22nd)

    Abstract: We discuss what information is encoded in a map of infinity-operads using only its definition. We give a definition of Day convolution on functor infinity-categories, and apply it to produce the smash product of pointed Kan complexes and the smash product of spectra.

  12. Ring spectra (Nov. 29th)

    Abstract: We define the notion of ring spectra.

Reference:

  1. J. F. Adams, Stable homotopy and generalised homology, Chicago Lectures in Mathematics, University of Chicago Press, Chicago, Ill.-London, 1974. MR 0402720
  2. Jacob Lurie, Higher topos theory, https://www.math.ias.edu/~lurie/papers/HTT.pdf, April 2017
  3. Jacob Lurie, Higher algebra, https://www.math.ias.edu/~lurie/papers/HA.pdf, September 18, 2017
  4. Jacob Lurie, Spectral algebraic geometry (under construction!), https://www.math.ias.edu/~lurie/papers/SAG-rootfile.pdf, February 3, 2018
entry.summary: The only speaker of this seminar is Yong-Gyu Choi. Incomplete notes are available here. List of talks: Motivating example (Aug. 30th) Abstract: This talk is the very first talk for a semester long series on higher algebra. Higher algebra is the study of algebraic objects in spaces, correcting abnormal behavior of classical homological algebra and incorporating spheres into algebra. In this talk, we will see a concrete example of a higher-algebraic structure on singular cochains, and what structures are needed to formalize such structures with concrete diagrams. We assume familiarity with algebraic topology, category theory and homological algebra (at the first year graduate level).

entry.title: Introduction to Algebraic K-theory
entry.content:
This seminar is supervised by Prof. Jinhyun Park. The only speaker is Yong-Gyu Choi. Notes are in construction.

List of talks:

  1. Introduction to algebraic \(K\)-theory and Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem (3/18)

    Abstract: Algebraic \(K\)-theory originated with the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem, a generalization of Riemann-Roch theorem to higher dimensional varieties. For this, we shall discuss the definitions of \(K_0\)-theory of a variety, its connection with intersection theory, \(\lambda\)-operation, \(\gamma\)-filtration, Chern classes and Adams operations.

  2. Quillen's higher algebraic \(K\)-theory I (4/1)

    Abstract: Grothendieck's \(K_0\)-group has an exact sequence \(K_0(Z)\to K_0(X)\text{}\to K_0(X\smallsetminus Z)\to 0\) for a closed immersion \(Z\to X\) of regular noetherian schemes, whose kernel of the leftmost map is usually nontrivial. To prolong this sequence functorially, Quillen defined higher algebraic \(K\)-groups as the higher homotopy groups of some mysterious category associated to an exact category. For this, we introduce simplicial sets, \(Q\)-construction of an exact category, higher \(K\)-groups of schemes, and their remarkable theorems, part of which extends the previously mentioned exact sequence for \(K_0\) and relates higher \(K\)-theory with Chow groups. This is the first part of Quillen's algebraic \(K\)-theory.

  3. Quillen's higher algebraic \(K\)-theory II (4/22)

    Abstract: Grothendieck's \(K_0\)-group has an exact sequence \(K_0(Z)\to K_0(X)\text{}\to K_0(X\smallsetminus Z)\to 0\) for a closed immersion \(Z\to X\) of regular noetherian schemes, whose kernel of the leftmost map is usually nontrivial. To prolong this sequence functorially, Quillen defined higher algebraic \(K\)-groups as the higher homotopy groups of some mysterious category associated to an exact category. For this, we introduce simplicial sets, \(Q\)-construction of an exact category, higher \(K\)-groups of schemes, and their remarkable theorems, part of which extends the previously mentioned exact sequence for \(K_0\) and relates higher \(K\)-theory with Chow groups. This is the second part of Quillen's algebraic \(K\)-theory.

  4. Quillen's higher algebraic \(K\)-theory III (5/13)

    Abstract: We defined Quillen's higher algebraic \(K\)-theory and examined its basic properties in previous talks. By the localization theorem and the dévissage theorem, the codimension filtration on \(\operatorname{Coh}(X)\) for a finite dimensional noetherian scheme \(X\) gives the Brown-Gersten-Quillen spectral sequence from page 1. If \(X\) is a regular algebraic scheme, then the second page of this spectral sequence is given by \(E_2^{p,-q}=H^p_{Zar}(X, \underline G_{q})\) and \(E_2^{p,-p}=CH^p(X)\), where \(\underline G_{q}\) denotes the Zariski sheafification of \(U\mapsto G_q(U)\). To prove this, we employ Quillen's geometric presentation lemma. This is the third and last part of Quillen's algebraic \(K\)-theory.

  5. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh I : Perfect complexes (5/27)

    Abstract: In the setting of Quillen’s \(K\)-theory, the \(K\)-groups are defined for the category of (algebraic) vector bundles. However, even in SGA 6, the need of replacing vector bundles by complexes quasi-isomorphic to bounded complexes of vector bundles was noticed. Such complexes are called perfect complexes and their \(K\)-theory provide better local-to-global properties, for example, Nisnevich descent or the localization sequence. In this talk, the basic notions of perfect complexes and pseudo-coherent complexes will be investigated and characterized as compact objects. We embrace as many details as possible.

  6. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh II : Waldhausen's \(S\)-construction (6/14)

    Abstract: Quillen's \(K\)-theory is defined for categories in which a suitable notion of exactness can be spoken. In much more generality, Waldhausen introduced the \(S\)-construction for categories equipped with suitable notions of cofibrations and weak equivalences. We will discuss the definition of \(S\)-construction, its dependence on the derived category, the cofinality theorem, and its comparison to Quillen's \(K\)-theory.

  7. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh III : Algebraic \(K\)-theory of schemes (6/28)

    Abstract: In previous talks, we investigated perfect complexes on a scheme and Waldhausen categories for which \(K\)-theory spectrum is defined. We'll define algebraic \(K\)-theory of quasi-compact quasi-separated schemes with these notions. Basic properties of algebraic \(K\)-theory will be discussed; properties include functoriality, excision property, relation with inverse limits and Poincaré duality. For this, we need various models of algebraic \(K\)-theory which give homotopy equivalent \(K\)-theory spectra most of which are proved by dependency of the derived categories of \(K\)-theory spectra.

  8. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh IV : The proto-localization theorem (7/26)

    Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U\) be its open subscheme. If \(X\) is noetherian, then any coherent sheaf on \(U\) always extends to \(X\). By contrast, extension problem of algebraic vector bundles is far from being true in this naive sense; there is a counterexample even for \((\mathbf A^3,\mathbf A^3\smallsetminus0)\). Nevertheless, if \(X\) is regular, then the Poincaré duality for \(K\)-theory shows that a coherent sheaf on \(X\) extending a given algebraic vector bundle on \(U\) is resolved by a bounded complex of algebraic vector bundles. Together with Waldhausen's resolution theorem stating that \(K\)-theory essentially depends on derived categories, this suggests that the right objects we should consider for this problem are perfect complexes. We will prove that the failure of extension of perfect complexes on \(U\) to \(X\) in the derived category is captured by the cokernel of \(K_0(X)\to K_0(U)\), which is proved by Thomason-Trobaugh. As an analogue to Quillen's localization theorem for \(G\)-theory of noetherian schemes, it then directly gives the proto-localization theorem for \(K\)-theory of quasi-compact quasi-separated schemes except that the proto-localization theorem doesn't have surjectivity of \(K_0(X)\to K_0(U)\). If possible, we will measure to what degree this map is surjective by introducing the non-connective Bass \(K\)-theory spectrum.

  9. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh V : Non-connective algebraic K-theory spectrum (9/17)

    Algebraic \(K\)-theory of schemes has a sequence of \(K\)-groups for a closed immersion which is exact except only one place, namely, \(K_0\) of the open immersion of the complement. Following the idea of Hyman Bass, we define a non-connective spectrum of a scheme whose non-negative part coincides with the usual algebraic \(K\)-theory defined by perfect complexes. This non-connective \(K\)-theory spectrum in particular gives a long exact sequence for a closed immersion. Our aim is to construct it, which is done by descending induction and requires the projective bundle formula that is also important regardless of our situation.

  10. Algebraic \(K\)-theory following Thomason-Trobaugh VI : Descent theorems (10/8)

    Recall that there is a spectral sequence strongly converging to the connective \(K\)-groups whose second page is given by the Zariski cohomology of connective \(K\)-theory sheaf. In the proof of this result by Quillen, the localization theorem is the most important ingredient. We prove an analogous statement for non-connective \(K\)-theory with both Zariski and Nisnevich cohomology for noetherian schemes of finite Krull dimension. This theorem is usually phrased as “non-connective algebraic \(K\)-theory satisfies Zariski and Nisnevich descent”. It is known that non-connective algebraic \(K\)-theory does not satisfy étale descent.

Reference:

  1. Yu. I. Manin, Lectures on the K-functor in Algebraic Geometry, Uspekhi Mat. Nauk 24:5 (1969), 3-86 (Russian); English transl. in Russian Math. Surveys 24:5 (1969), 1-89.
  2. D. Quillen, Higher algebraic K-theory: I, Lecture Notes in Math., vol. 341, Springer-Verlag, 1973, pp. 85–147.
  3. R. W. Thomason and T. Trobaugh, Higher algebraic K-theory of schemes and of derived categories, The Grothendieck Festschrift III, Progress in Math., vol. 88, Birkhäuser, 1990, pp. 247–435.
  4. P. Berthelot, A. Grothendieck and L. Illusie, Théorie des intersections et théorème de Riemann-Roch, Lecture Notes in Math., vol. 225, Springer, 1971.
  5. R. W. Thomason, Algebraic \(K\)-theory and etale cohomology, Ann. Sci. École Norm. Sup. (4) 18 (1985), no. 3, 437–552.
entry.summary: This seminar is supervised by Prof. Jinhyun Park. The only speaker is Yong-Gyu Choi. Notes are in construction. List of talks: Introduction to algebraic \(K\)-theory and Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem (3/18) Abstract: Algebraic \(K\)-theory originated with the Grothendieck-Riemann-Roch theorem, a generalization of Riemann-Roch theorem to higher dimensional varieties. For this, we shall discuss the definitions of \(K_0\)-theory of a variety, its connection with intersection theory, \(\lambda\)-operation, \(\gamma\)-filtration, Chern classes and Adams operations.
